skill.name: api-fuzzing-bug-bounty
skill.description: Guides authorized API security testing for REST, SOAP, and GraphQL targets, including endpoint discovery, IDOR checks, injection testing, and reporting workflows.
skill.summary: Plan and document authorized API fuzzing and bug bounty tests.
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: Static command-execution, network, filesystem, and weak-crypto matches are mostly fenced examples, not executable skill code. However, semantic review confirms high-risk dual-use content: the skill gives actionable API exploitation, unauthorized data access, DoS, SSRF, local file read, and rate-limit evasion techniques. No prompt injection attempt or hidden exfiltration logic was found in SKILL.md.

high_findings.title: Actionable API Exploitation Guidance
high_findings.description: TRUE_POSITIVE semantic risk. The skill provides step-by-step techniques for IDOR, authentication bypass, command injection, XXE, SSRF, GraphQL data access, and endpoint bypass testing. The material can support legitimate bug bounty work, but it is directly actionable for unauthorized exploitation if used outside an approved scope.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited sections contain concrete payloads and workflows for accessing other users data, injecting commands, reading local files, reaching localhost services, and stressing API limits. The purpose text frames the material as bug bounty testing, so this is high-risk dual-use rather than confirmed malware.

high_findings.title: Rate-Limit and Access-Control Evasion Guidance
high_findings.description: TRUE_POSITIVE semantic risk. The skill instructs users to probe missing rate limits, batch GraphQL login attempts, alter denied endpoint paths, and use IP rotation when rate limited. These instructions can facilitate account attacks or abuse of production APIs.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: The guidance explicitly targets bypassing limits and denied access paths, and line 434 recommends IP rotation. Legitimate testing is possible, but the abuse potential is clear.

medium_findings.title: Dangerous Payload Examples in Documentation
medium_findings.description: TRUE_POSITIVE content risk, FALSE_POSITIVE for local execution. Static findings for system file access, localhost URLs, and command payloads refer to examples inside Markdown fences, not code executed by the skill. The examples still teach XXE local file reads, PDF-based file access, SSRF, port scanning, and command injection patterns.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file shows non-executable examples, so the static execution concern is mostly false positive. The same examples are concrete exploitation payloads, which creates a real content safety concern.

medium_findings.title: External Command Detections Are Markdown Examples
medium_findings.description: FALSE_POSITIVE for hidden command execution. The many Ruby or shell backtick detections are caused by fenced code blocks and inline test payloads in SKILL.md. No separate script, installer, or runtime file executes these commands automatically.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: Review found only one Markdown file and the cited lines are documentation examples. The risk remains user-directed execution against external targets, not automatic command execution by the skill.

low_findings.title: Weak-Crypto and XMLHttpRequest Matches Are Scanner Noise
low_findings.description: FALSE_POSITIVE. The weak cryptographic algorithm findings appear to match ordinary text or GraphQL schema strings, not cryptographic implementation. XMLHttpRequest appears as a suggested request header value, not browser code that sends data.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines contain prose, GraphQL introspection text, a table heading, or a header name. I found no cryptographic code or XMLHttpRequest API call in SKILL.md.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded URLs and IP Addresses Are Test Placeholders
low_findings.description: FALSE_POSITIVE for exfiltration endpoints. The hardcoded domains, localhost URLs, and private IP style targets are presented as testing placeholders or payload examples. No evidence found of a hidden callback, credential upload, or automatic network request.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URLs are inside examples and no executable code performs outbound requests. One iplogger example is concerning as guidance, but it is not a concealed exfiltration mechanism.

dangerous_patterns.title: Unauthorized Object Access Testing Patterns
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill includes examples for changing object identifiers and requesting another users invoice or profile data. This is a normal authorized test case, but it is high impact when used without permission.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The examples directly describe changing identifiers to access other users resources. The risk depends on authorization and testing scope.

dangerous_patterns.title: SSRF, Local File Read, and Port-Scanning Payloads
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill includes localhost SSRF payloads, file URI payloads for local file reads, and image tags aimed at internal ports. These are sensitive techniques that can expose internal services or files.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The cited payloads explicitly target local files and localhost services. They are documentation examples, but the exploitation pattern is clear.

dangerous_patterns.title: Denial-of-Service and Rate-Limit Bypass Patterns
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill suggests nested GraphQL queries, excessive limit values, batching login attempts, and IP rotation after rate limiting. These patterns can disrupt services or enable abuse.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The cited content describes load amplification and evasion approaches. It does not execute them automatically, but it is actionable operational guidance.

content.user_title: Test APIs for Bug Bounty Findings
content.value_statement: API security testing can miss authorization, injection, and GraphQL flaws. This skill gives structured checklists and payload ideas for authorized bug bounty assessments.
actual_capabilities: Outlines REST, SOAP, and GraphQL API testing workflows.
Lists endpoint discovery methods and API documentation checks.
Provides checklists for IDOR, injection, SSRF, and method tampering tests.
Suggests GraphQL introspection, batching, and nested query review areas.
Helps organize bug bounty evidence and reproduction notes.
limitations: It is documentation only and does not run automated scans.
It requires explicit authorization and a defined testing scope.
It does not verify findings or determine exploitability by itself.
Some techniques may be unsafe for production systems without rate limits.

use_cases.title: Bug Bounty API Triage
use_cases.description: Plan scoped checks for API authorization, hidden endpoints, and version differences before submitting a report.

use_cases.title: Internal API Review
use_cases.description: Use the checklist to review REST and GraphQL services before a security assessment or launch.

use_cases.title: Security Training Lab
use_cases.description: Teach common API vulnerability classes using controlled lab targets and documented examples.

prompt_templates.title: Create a Scoped API Test Plan
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a safe API security test plan for this authorized target. Include REST, GraphQL, authentication, IDOR, and rate-limit checks.

prompt_templates.title: Review API Documentation for Risks
prompt_templates.prompt: Review these API routes and documentation. Identify likely authorization, versioning, input validation, and sensitive data exposure risks.

prompt_templates.title: Design GraphQL Test Cases
prompt_templates.prompt: Build a GraphQL security test checklist for this schema. Focus on authorization boundaries, introspection exposure, batching, and query depth limits.

prompt_templates.title: Prepare a Bug Bounty Report
prompt_templates.prompt: Convert these authorized API test notes into a clear bug bounty report with impact, reproduction steps, evidence, and remediation guidance.

output_examples.input: Plan testing for a new customer billing API.
output_examples.output: A scoped test plan covering authentication, object ownership checks, versioned endpoints, sensitive invoice data, and safe rate-limit validation.

output_examples.input: Review notes from a GraphQL endpoint assessment.
output_examples.output: A prioritized finding summary that separates schema exposure, missing field authorization, batching risk, and query depth concerns.

output_examples.input: Turn API fuzzing notes into a report.
output_examples.output: A bug bounty style report with title, scope, impact, reproduction narrative, observed evidence, severity rationale, and remediation steps.

best_practices: Confirm written authorization, target scope, and testing windows before using any technique.
Use controlled request rates and avoid destructive payloads on production systems.
Document evidence, affected roles, expected behavior, and remediation steps clearly.
anti_patterns: Testing APIs without permission or outside the approved scope.
Using denial-of-service, brute-force, or IP rotation techniques against live users.
Submitting raw payload dumps without impact analysis or clear reproduction steps.

faq.question: Does this skill run API fuzzing tools automatically?
faq.answer: No. It is a written guide and checklist. Users must run any tools themselves within an authorized scope.

faq.question: Is this suitable for production testing?
faq.answer: Only with approval, rate controls, and a defined testing window. Some techniques can affect service availability.

faq.question: Can it help with GraphQL security reviews?
faq.answer: Yes. It covers introspection, authorization boundaries, batching, nested queries, and schema discovery considerations.

faq.question: Does it replace a professional penetration test?
faq.answer: No. It supports planning and documentation, but it does not validate findings or provide legal authorization.

faq.question: Why is the marketplace risk rating high?
faq.answer: The guide includes actionable exploitation and evasion techniques. It is useful for authorized work but risky if misused.

faq.question: What should users prepare before using it?
faq.answer: Prepare written authorization, target URLs, API documentation, test accounts, rate limits, and reporting requirements.
